target.name: Chloromelodinine E
target.motivation: Chloromelodinine E (1) was recently discovered in 2024 and exhibits selective cytotoxicity toward human ovarian cancer cells. A concise and unified approach to prepare various leuconoxine-type alkaloids, particularly those with a fully substituted pyrrolidinone ring, is highly desirable, and total synthesis of 1 has not been reported yet.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: early installation of the C18 quaternary center, followed by formation of the indole scaffold, and an end game with the pyrrolidone construction via an Aldol condensation

success_criteria.free_text: Concise and unified approach to prepare various leuconoxine-type alkaloids, particularly those with a fully substituted pyrrolidinone ring. First total synthesis of chloromelodinine E.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming to construct the B/C rings and the C18 quaternary center
key_disconnections.reasoning: This method offers a modular synthetic approach to access multi-substituted pyrroles from simple starting materials, avoiding the need for additional handles like halides.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-N bond forming to construct the macrolactam
key_disconnections.reasoning: Macrolactam 10 serves as a common intermediate for divergent synthesis of several leuconoxine-type alkaloids.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Oxidative dearomatization of the pyrrole ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: Dearomative transformations of a multi-substituted pyrrole could offer rapid access to the complex pyrrolidinone B ring in leuconoxine alkaloids.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy is centered on efficient early construction of a poly-substituted pyrrole core structure using palladium/norbornene (Pd/NBE) cooperative catalysis. This pyrrole-centered approach is distinct from prior strategies that focused on constructing the indoline core first. A key feature is the strategic use of a chloro substituent, which enhances the efficiency of the Pd/NBE-catalyzed double C-H functionalization, increases pyrrole intermediate stability, guides site-selectivity during late-stage oxidative dearomatization, provides the desired oxidation state for the C5 carbon, and can be transferred to the C6 position for chloromelodinine synthesis. The macrolactam 10 serves as a common intermediate for divergent synthesis of various leuconoxine-type alkaloids via oxidative dearomatization.

thought.background: Leuconoxine-type alkaloids are a large class of structurally complex natural products with diverse biological activities. Prior syntheses have focused on constructing the indoline core or using indole-derived starting materials.
thought.gap: A concise and unified approach to prepare various leuconoxine-type alkaloids, particularly those with a fully substituted pyrrolidinone ring, is needed. Total synthesis of chloromelodinine E has not been reported yet.
thought.decision: To address the limitations of prior syntheses, a pyrrole-centered strategy will be pursued, focusing on efficient early construction of a poly-substituted pyrrole core structure using palladium/norbornene (Pd/NBE) cooperative catalysis.

outcome.observation: Identified the need for a new synthetic strategy for leuconoxine-type alkaloids, especially chloromelodinine E.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, a concise and unified approach to prepare various leuconoxine-type alkaloids, particularly those with a fully substituted pyrrolidinone ring, would be highly desirable. Here, we describe our strategy for divergent total syntheses of chloromelodinine (1), leuconodine A (2), leuconodine F (3), melodinine E (4), and leuconoxine (5), which is centered on efficient early construction of a poly-substituted pyrrole core structure using the palladium/norbornene (Pd/NBE) cooperative catalysis.

thought.background: Commercially available pyrrole 13 is the starting material. A ketone 19 is needed for the next step.
thought.gap: How to synthesize ketone 19 from pyrrole 13 efficiently and on a gram scale.
thought.decision: To synthesize ketone 19, an in situ Grignard reagent formation from 13 followed by addition to known Weinreb amide 18 will be used, expecting high yield and gram-scale production.

outcome.observation: The reaction gave high yield on gram scale, and the tert-butyl ester group in 18 was intact.

evidence.text_span: the total synthesis commenced with synthesis of ketone 19 from commercially available pyrrole 13,[42] which involved in situ formation of the Grignard reagent from 13 followed by addition to known Weinreb amide 18[43] (Scheme 2a). The reaction gave high yield on gram scale, and the tertbutyl ester group in 18 was intact.

thought.gap: How to introduce an olefin moiety into ketone 19.
thought.decision: Wittig olefination of the ketone moiety will be performed using potassium tert-butoxide as the base and toluene as the solvent, expecting a smooth reaction and that the resulting E/Z isomer mixture can be converted into the same product in the later Pd/NBE catalysis.

outcome.observation: Wittig olefination of the ketone moiety proceeded smoothly, yielding olefin 20 as an E/Z isomer mixture.

evidence.text_span: Next, Wittig olefination of the ketone moiety proceeded smoothly using potassium tertbutoxide as the base and toluene as the solvent. The resulting E/Z isomer mixture of olefin 20 could be converted into the same product in the later Pd/NBE catalysis.

thought.gap: How to introduce a chloro substituent at the 2-position of the pyrrole ring.
thought.decision: Treatment of olefin 20 with N-chlorosuccinimide (NCS) will be used to produce 2-chloro-substituted pyrrole 21, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: 2-chloro-substituted pyrrole 21 was produced in 71% yield, along with 12% recovery of the starting material.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of olefin 20 with N.-chlorosuccinimide (NCS) produced 2- chloro-substituted pyrrole 21 in 71% yield, along with 12% recovery of the starting material.

thought.background: Chloropyrrole 21 is ready for the key difunctionalization. The Pd/NBE-catalyzed β-arylation/intramolecular α-Heck reaction is crucial for constructing the B/C rings and the C18 quaternary center.
thought.gap: How to achieve the desired β-arylation/intramolecular α-Heck reaction with chloropyrrole 21 in good yield and scalability.
thought.decision: The reaction conditions will be further optimized for better yield and scalability. Ortho-nitrophenyl iodide will be used as the electrophile, and simple NBE (N1) will be tested as it worked better than N6 in this case, likely due to less steric hindrance allowing stronger coordination with Pd, minimizing intramolecular Heck side-reactions.

outcome.observation: The desired β-arylation/intramolecular α-Heck reaction was achieved with the chloropyrrole substrate (21) to afford bicycle 11 in decent yield. Simple NBE (N1) worked better than N6.

evidence.text_span: The stage was set for the key vicinal double C-H functionalization of pyrrole to construct B/C rings and the C18 quaternary center. After further optimization of the reaction conditions for better yield and scalability (see Supporting Information for details), the desired β-arylation/intramolecular α-Heck reaction was achieved with the chloropyrrole substrate (21) to afford bicycle 11 in decent yield. Ortho-nitrophenyl iodide was used as the electrophile, and simple NBE (N1) worked better than N6 in this case, likely because the less sterically hindered N1 can coordinate with Pd more strongly so that the intramolecular Heck side-reaction can be minimized.

thought.gap: How to reduce the nitro group and the double bond in 11 to obtain aniline 22, while maintaining the chloro group and avoiding decomposition.
thought.decision: A simultaneous reduction of the nitro group and the double bond will be attempted.

outcome.observation: Simultaneous reduction of the nitro group and the double bond proved challenging due to the competing reactivity of the chloride moiety. The de-chlorinated product would become less stable and susceptible toward decomposition.

evidence.text_span: With a sufficient quantity of bicycle 11 in hand, while simultaneous reduction of the nitro group and the double bond proved challenging due to the competing reactivity of the chloride moiety, a carefully optimized two-step sequence successfully afforded aniline 22. Keeping the chloro group proved to be critical because the de-chlorinated product would become less stable and susceptible toward decomposition.

thought.background: Simultaneous reduction of the nitro group and the double bond in 11 failed due to decomposition and instability of the de-chlorinated product.
thought.gap: How to selectively reduce the nitro group and the double bond while preserving the chloro group and ensuring stability.
thought.decision: A carefully optimized two-step sequence will be employed to successfully afford aniline 22, ensuring the chloro group is kept as it is critical for stability.

outcome.observation: A carefully optimized two-step sequence successfully afforded aniline 22, keeping the chloro group intact.

evidence.text_span: With a sufficient quantity of bicycle 11 in hand, while simultaneous reduction of the nitro group and the double bond proved challenging due to the competing reactivity of the chloride moiety, a carefully optimized two-step sequence successfully afforded aniline 22. Keeping the chloro group proved to be critical because the de-chlorinated product would become less stable and susceptible toward decomposition.

thought.gap: How to convert aniline 22 into macrolactam 10.
thought.decision: Hydrolysis of the ester will be performed, followed by macrolactamization under mild conditions, expecting a good overall yield for the two steps.

outcome.observation: Hydrolysis of the ester yielded the corresponding carboxylic acid uneventfully, which then underwent macrolactamization under mild conditions to afford macrolactam 10 in 82% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, hydrolysis of the ester yielded the corresponding carboxylic acid uneventfully, which then underwent macrolactamization under mild conditions to afford macrolactam 10 in 82% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Macrolactam 10 is a common intermediate for divergent synthesis.
thought.gap: How to synthesize melodinine E (4) from macrolactam 10.
thought.decision: Treatment of 10 with purified m-CPBA in anhydrous DCM will be used to afford melodinine E (4).

outcome.observation: Melodinine E (4) was afforded in 71% yield.

evidence.text_span: First, simply treatment of 10 with purified m-CPBA in anhydrous DCM afforded melodinine E (4) in 71% yield.

thought.gap: How to synthesize leuconoxine (5) from melodinine E (4).
thought.decision: Diastereoselective hydrogenation of the alkene moiety in 4 will be performed following a reported condition, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: The diastereoselective hydrogenation of the alkene moiety in 4 completed the total synthesis of leuconoxine (5) in 90% yield.

evidence.text_span: The subsequent diastereoselective hydrogenation of the alkene moiety in 4, following a reported condition,[11] completed the total synthesis of leuconoxine (5) in 90% yield.

thought.background: Macrolactam 10 is a common intermediate for divergent synthesis.
thought.gap: How to synthesize chloro-1,2-migration compound 24 and leuconolam (23) from macrolactam 10.
thought.decision: Treatment of macrolactam 10 with excess freshly prepared dimethyldioxirane (DMDO) in acetone at low temperature will be used, expecting to yield chloro-1,2-migration compound 24 along with some leuconolam (23).

outcome.observation: Macrolactam 10 yielded the chloro-1,2-migration (C5 to C6) compound 24, along with some leuconolam (23). The structure of 24 was unambiguously characterized by X-ray crystallography.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, treating macrolactam 10 with excess freshly prepared dimethyldioxirane (DMDO) in acetone at low temperature yielded the chloro-1,2-migration (C5 to C6) compound 24, along with some leuconolam (23). The structure of 24 was unambiguously characterized by X-ray crystallography.

thought.gap: How to synthesize chloromelodinine (1) and leuconodine F (3) from compound 24.
thought.decision: Allowing 24 to react in a TFA/DCM solution will promote the transannular cyclization to furnish chloromelodinine (1) and leuconodine F (3).

outcome.observation: Transannular cyclization furnished chloromelodinine (1) in 49% yield and leuconodine F (3) in 12% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, allowing 24 to react in a TFA/DCM solution promoted the transannular cyclization to furnish chloromelodinine (1) in 49% yield and leuconodine F (3) in 12% yield.

thought.gap: How to synthesize leuconodine A (2) from leuconolam (23).
thought.decision: Leuconodine A (2) will be obtained from leuconolam (23) under similar conditions to the transannular cyclization, based on a reported mechanism.

outcome.observation: Leuconodine A (2) was obtained in 67% yield from leuconolam (23).

evidence.text_span: Under similar conditions, leuconodine A (2) was obtained in 67% yield from leuconolam (23), based on a reported mechanism.

thought.background: Macrolactam 10 is a common intermediate for divergent synthesis.
thought.gap: How to synthesize leuconodine F (3) directly from macrolactam 10 in a one-pot manner.
thought.decision: Leuconodine F (3) will be synthesized in a one-pot manner from macrolactam 10 by merging the oxidative dearomatization and transannular cyclization processes, expecting a good yield.

outcome.observation: Leuconodine F (3) could also be synthesized in a one-pot manner from macrolactam 10 in good yield through merging these processes.

evidence.text_span: Furthermore, leuconodine F (3) could also be synthesized in a one-pot manner from macrolactam 10 in good yield through merging these processes (Scheme 2b).

thought.gap: How to synthesize 6-epi-leuconodine A (25) from leuconodine F (3).
thought.decision: NaBH4 reduction of the ketone moiety in 3 will be performed to lead to the formation of 6-epi-leuconodine A (25).

outcome.observation: NaBH4 reduction of the ketone moiety led to the formation of 6-epi-leuconodine A (25).

evidence.text_span: With sufficient 3 in hand, NaBH4 reduction of the ketone moiety led to the formation of 6-epi-leuconodine A (25), which was then successfully converted to natural product 2 using the Mitsunobu/hydrolysis protocol.

thought.gap: How to convert 6-epi-leuconodine A (25) to natural product leuconodine A (2).
thought.decision: The Mitsunobu/hydrolysis protocol will be used to convert 25 to 2.

outcome.observation: 6-epi-leuconodine A (25) was successfully converted to natural product 2 using the Mitsunobu/hydrolysis protocol.

evidence.text_span: With sufficient 3 in hand, NaBH4 reduction of the ketone moiety led to the formation of 6-epi-leuconodine A (25), which was then successfully converted to natural product 2 using the Mitsunobu/hydrolysis protocol.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated for the entire synthesis, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The longest linear sequence to the target molecule.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of steps in the synthesis.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.comparison_to_prior: This work represents the shortest syntheses of these natural products to date with 10–11 total steps. The pyrrole-centered strategy is distinct from previous efforts that focused on constructing the indoline part first.
validation.significance: The work provides concise total syntheses of five leuconoxine-type alkaloids, including the first total synthesis of chloromelodinine E. It features a newly developed palladium/norbornene-catalyzed pyrrole double C-H functionalization, a divergent oxidative dearomatization, and strategic use of a chloro substituent. The discovery of an unusual chloro 1,2-migration reaction is also significant.

source.title: Concise Total Syntheses of Leuconoxine-Type Alkaloids Enabled by Palladium/Norbornene-Catalyzed Pyrrole Difunctionalization.
source.authors: Liu X, Zhou Y, Meng Y, Zhu Q, Li R, Dong G.
